claims.claim_text: Falco was created by Sysdig in 2016 and entered CNCF in 2018.
claims.rationale: The CNCF announcement says Falco was created and open sourced in 2016 by Sysdig and entered CNCF Sandbox in 2018.

claims.claim_text: Falco was one of the first container runtime security tools and is now used by thousands of organizations.
claims.anchor_text: It was one of the first tools designed specifically for container runtime security and is now used by thousands of organizations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Falco is a CNCF runtime security project used to detect suspicious behavior in cloud native workloads.
claims.rationale: Allowlisted sources support Falco's history, but not this superlative claim or the unverified adoption count.

claims.claim_text: The Linux capability system has 41 distinct capabilities as of kernel 5.x.
claims.rationale: This is a real kernel-detail claim, but the most direct primary reference is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: CAP_SYS_ADMIN grants about 30% of all privileged operations and is called the new root.
claims.anchor_text: `CAP_SYS_ADMIN` alone grants about 30% of all privileged operations—which is why it's called "the new root."
claims.suggested_rewrite: `CAP_SYS_ADMIN` is unusually broad, which is why it is often treated as "the new root."
claims.rationale: The exact percentage is not directly supportable from an allowlisted primary source, but the broader lesson is valid.

claims.claim_text: CVE-2019-5736 in runc allowed host-level compromise and required runtime patching.
claims.anchor_text: 3. **The first major container escape (CVE-2019-5736)** in runc allowed attackers to overwrite the host's runc binary and escape any container. It affected Docker, Kubernetes, containerd, and more. Patching required updating the container runtime, not just Kubernetes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **CVE-2019-5736 in runc** allowed attackers to overwrite the host's `runc` binary and gain host-level access in affected environments, so remediation centered on patching the container runtime rather than only Kubernetes.
claims.rationale: NVD supports the runc overwrite vulnerability, but the original wording overclaims with an unsupported superlative and an overbroad affected-product list.

claims.claim_text: NetworkPolicy resources have no effect unless the cluster uses a network plugin that enforces NetworkPolicy.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes documentation explicitly says creating a NetworkPolicy has no effect without a controller or plugin that implements NetworkPolicy enforcement.

claims.claim_text: An e-commerce company saw cluster costs double overnight because of a runtime compromise.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Cryptominer at 3 AM
claims.anchor_text: An e-commerce company noticed their Kubernetes cluster costs had doubled overnight.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A compromised workload can drive a sudden spike in cluster CPU usage and cost.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident anecdote with specific operational impact and no citable source.

claims.claim_text: A vulnerable npm package enabled remote code execution in the compromised workload.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Cryptominer at 3 AM / The Root Cause
claims.anchor_text: A vulnerable npm package allowed remote code execution.
claims.suggested_rewrite: One common path to this kind of compromise is an application vulnerability or vulnerable dependency that enables code execution.
claims.rationale: The module presents a specific root cause for a real-looking incident without attribution.

claims.claim_text: The attacker exploited an RCE in this specific incident.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Cryptominer at 3 AM / The Root Cause
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. Gained code execution in the workload
claims.rationale: This is a concrete incident step tied to an uncited anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The attacker downloaded xmrig to /tmp in this specific incident.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Cryptominer at 3 AM / The Root Cause
claims.suggested_rewrite: 2. Dropped a miner or other unauthorized binary into a writable path
claims.rationale: The named malware and filesystem path are presented as incident facts without a source.

claims.claim_text: The attacker started cryptocurrency mining in this specific incident.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Cryptominer at 3 AM / The Root Cause
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. Began abusing the workload's compute resources
claims.rationale: This is another specific uncited incident step that should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: The company paid the compute bill while the attacker kept the proceeds.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Cryptominer at 3 AM / The Root Cause
claims.anchor_text: 4. Company paid for compute, attacker got the crypto
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. The defender absorbs the infrastructure cost while the attacker captures the benefit
claims.rationale: This is a specific outcome claim from an unattributed war story.

claims.claim_text: The same attack happened again two weeks later.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Cryptominer at 3 AM / After implementing
claims.anchor_text: - Same attack attempt happened 2 weeks later
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Similar repeat attempts are easier to catch once runtime controls are in place
claims.rationale: This is a dated follow-up incident claim with no attribution.

claims.claim_text: Falco detected the repeat attack in 15 seconds.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Cryptominer at 3 AM / After implementing
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Runtime detection can surface suspicious behavior quickly
claims.rationale: The exact response time is an uncited incident metric.

claims.claim_text: An automatic Slack alert fired in the repeat incident.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Cryptominer at 3 AM / After implementing
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Automated alerting can notify responders immediately
claims.rationale: This is a specific operational event in an unattributed anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The compromised pod was killed before meaningful mining occurred.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Cryptominer at 3 AM / After implementing
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Rapid containment can limit the attacker's dwell time and resource abuse
claims.rationale: This is an uncited claim about a specific containment outcome.

claims.claim_text: The post-mortem identified and patched the vulnerable package.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Cryptominer at 3 AM / After implementing
claims.anchor_text: - Post-mortem: Found the vulnerable package, patched it
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Post-incident review should identify the root cause and feed remediation back into the build and deployment process
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed post-incident conclusion presented as a real event.

further_reading.title: Pod Security Standards
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative reference for the privileged, baseline, and restricted profiles discussed in the module.

further_reading.title: Network Policies
further_reading.why_relevant: Defines how Kubernetes NetworkPolicy works and when policy resources do or do not take effect.

further_reading.title: Seccomp and Kubernetes
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains seccomp profile types, inheritance rules, and runtime behavior for Kubernetes workloads.

further_reading.title: Falco
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides the official CNCF project overview for Falco, which is central to the runtime detection portion of the module.

notes: The fabricated cryptominer war story contains additional uncited timeline and terminal-output specifics inside code fences. The schema guidance says code blocks should not emit claims, so those lines are not individually seeded here; manual editorial cleanup of that section may still be warranted.
